input: Red team the amyloid hypothesis for Alzheimer's disease. The claim: 'Clearing amyloid beta plaques will slow cognitive decline in Alzheimer's patients.'

key_vulnerabilities: decades of clinical trial failures (aducanumab controversy, solanezumab failure)
amyloid clearance doesn't correlate well with cognitive improvement
tau pathology correlates better with cognition than amyloid
many elderly have amyloid plaques without dementia
lecanemab shows statistical but questionable clinical significance (CDR-SB 0.45 point difference)
expected_details.should_acknowledge_recent_data: lecanemab/donanemab show modest benefit but don't fully validate hypothesis

input: Red team the safety profile of in vivo CRISPR gene editing for sickle cell disease. Assume systemic AAV delivery of Cas9.

key_vulnerabilities: off-target editing at unintended genomic loci
pre-existing AAV antibodies in ~40-70% of population
AAV immunogenicity and hepatotoxicity
genotoxicity from double-strand breaks
inability to redose (anti-AAV immune memory)
mosaicism (incomplete editing across target cells)
hidden_assumptions: Cas9 specificity is sufficient at therapeutic doses
immune response won't eliminate edited cells

input: Red team this claim: 'Imatinib is an effective treatment for CML with BCR-ABL fusion.' This should score LOW severity — it's a well-validated, proven therapy.

expected_details.rationale: overwhelming Phase 3 evidence, 20+ years of clinical experience, multiple confirmatory studies

input: Red team: 'The gut microbiome composition determines response to immune checkpoint inhibitors, and fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) can convert non-responders to responders.'

key_vulnerabilities: correlation vs causation not established in large trials
microbiome composition highly variable and hard to standardize
FMT studies are small (N<30), single-center, unblinded
what defines a 'favorable' microbiome differs across studies
confounders: diet, antibiotics, geography, genetics
hidden_assumptions: microbiome is a modifiable determinant not a biomarker
favorable taxa are the same across cancer types

input: Red team this clinical trial design: Phase 3 superiority trial of Drug X vs placebo in HCC. Primary endpoint: OS. N=500. HR=0.75. One interim analysis at 50%. Crossover permitted at progression.

key_vulnerabilities: crossover dilutes OS treatment effect
HR 0.75 may be optimistic for HCC
N=500 likely underpowered if crossover dilution occurs
single interim may miss early futility signal
no co-primary PFS endpoint
mitigation_suggestions: increase N to account for dilution

input: Red team: 'AI-discovered drugs will reach market faster and cheaper than traditional approaches, reducing drug development timelines from 10-15 years to 3-5 years.'

key_vulnerabilities: AI accelerates hit finding but doesn't compress clinical trial timelines (biology is rate-limiting)
no AI-discovered drug has completed Phase 3 from start to finish yet
clinical trial duration is regulatory-constrained not computation-constrained
training data bias limits generalizability
survivorship bias in reported AI successes
hidden_assumptions: preclinical speed translates to clinical speed
AI predictions are accurate enough to skip experimental validation steps

input: Red team this regulatory strategy: Seeking accelerated approval for a novel Alzheimer's drug based on amyloid PET clearance as surrogate endpoint. Phase 2 data shows 30% amyloid reduction at 18 months.

key_vulnerabilities: amyloid PET clearance not accepted as validated surrogate (FDA Aduhelm controversy)
30% reduction is modest compared to lecanemab (~60%)
no cognitive endpoint data to support
confirmatory trial requirement creates substantial risk
advisory committee likely skeptical given Aduhelm precedent
hidden_assumptions: FDA will accept amyloid clearance as reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit
confirmatory trial can be completed in reasonable timeframe

input: Red team: 'Whole genome sequencing for all newborns will prevent most genetic diseases through early intervention.'

key_vulnerabilities: most genetic variants are VUS — creating anxiety without actionability
many genetic diseases have no intervention even if detected early
psychological harm from incidental findings
cost-effectiveness not demonstrated at population scale
false positive rate at genome-wide scale
health equity concerns (who gets access?)
hidden_assumptions: early detection = better outcomes for all genetic conditions
healthcare system can act on findings
parents want to know about all genetic risks

input: Red team: 'Liquid biopsy (ctDNA) will completely replace tissue biopsy for cancer diagnosis within 5 years.'

key_vulnerabilities: ctDNA sensitivity limited for early-stage and low-tumor-burden cancers
tissue still needed for histology, grading, IHC markers
false negatives in ctDNA can miss actionable mutations
clonal hematopoiesis confounds ctDNA interpretation
regulatory infrastructure not ready for liquid-only diagnosis
expected_details.nuance: liquid biopsy will SUPPLEMENT not REPLACE tissue biopsy

input: Red team a novel oral KRAS G12D inhibitor entering Phase 1. Preclinical data: IC50 15 nM, oral bioavailability 45% in mice, clean hERG (>30 μM). PDAC xenograft TGI 65%. No prior clinical KRAS G12D inhibitors approved.

key_vulnerabilities: mouse PK may not translate to human (species-specific metabolism)
TGI 65% is modest (not complete regression)
first-in-class G12D risk — no clinical precedent for dosing/efficacy
PDAC has historically poor response to targeted therapy
resistance mechanisms unknown for G12D class
competitive risk from MRTX1133 and other G12D programs
mitigations_expected: human PK prediction via allometric scaling
